concise:
Ayah 1–7 · linked video: 01. Al-Ma’un

Surah Al-Ma’un: Understanding True Worship Through Social Justice

Introduction to the Surah

Surah Al-Ma’un, also known as Surah Ad-Deen, derives its name from the first ayah which begins with أَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى يُكَذِّبُ بِٱلدِّينِ. This short but profound surah consists of only seven ayahs, yet from a thematic perspective, it divides distinctly into two halves that at first seem unrelated but are actually intricately connected.

The structural division becomes apparent in the Arabic grammar itself. The surah begins in the singular form (الذي), then halfway through shifts to the plural (الذين). This grammatical transition marks the surah’s two distinct yet complementary sections.

Historical Context

There is scholarly debate about whether this surah is Makki or Madani. Some scholars suggest it might be Madani because it discusses people who pray but show off (yura’oon), a characteristic often associated with the hypocrites of Madinah. The reasoning goes that hypocrisy emerged as a phenomenon primarily in Madinah, where there were worldly advantages to appearing Muslim - political benefits, social status, and alignment with the winning side. In contrast, accepting Islam in Makkah brought only persecution, torture, exile, and social ostracization. There was no worldly motivation to feign belief.

However, a deeper analysis suggests the entire surah is actually Makki. The language (عَن صَلَاتِهِمْ سَاهُونَ) - “about their prayer they are heedless” - uses the possessive form “their prayer,” which accurately describes how the Quraysh had their own modified version of ritual prayer. Even in that corrupted form of worship, they were superficial and insincere. This reading maintains the surah’s unified narrative as one comprehensive criticism.

Understanding this as a Makki surah makes the message even more powerful and dangerous. When these ayahs were revealed and recited, the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ had no army, no security detail, and no tribal protection beyond the aging Abu Talib. Yet here he stood, openly criticizing the leaders and custodians of the Kaaba, the most powerful people in Makkan society. The Quran’s majestic speech and rhythmic quality meant these verses spread like wildfire through the valley - فَسَالَتۡ أَوۡدِیَةُۢ بِقَدَرِهَا - “the valley was flooded as much as it could hold.” Everyone heard these criticisms echoing through Makkah.

This historical reality is crucial for understanding the Quran. The original experience of these ayahs was life-threatening. Reciting them could get you killed, or at minimum severely beaten. To truly appreciate the weight of these words, we must place ourselves back in that struggle, relive that history when we recite them today.

Connection to the Previous Surah

In Surah Quraysh, Allah ﷻ commanded: (فَلْيَعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّ هَٰذَا ٱلْبَيْتِ) - “Let them worship the Lord of this House.” The choice of words is significant. Allah ﷻ didn’t simply say “worship Allah” generically. He specifically said they should worship the Master of the Kaaba. Why? Because their unique position as custodians of Allah’s House came with unique responsibilities.

When Allah ﷻ compels a particular group of people - those blessed with safety, security, and economic prosperity centered around the Kaaba - to become His worshippers, that call to worship takes on a particular meaning. Surah Al-Ma’un essentially defines what that worship means by highlighting everything the Quraysh are NOT doing. These criticisms, if addressed, would constitute true servitude to Allah ﷻ. Their failure to address them proves they are not truly servants of Allah ﷻ, despite being in the service of His House.

Ayah 1

أَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى يُكَذِّبُ بِٱلدِّينِ

“Have you seen the one who denies the Recompense?”

The Rhetorical Question

The ayah opens with ara’ayta - “Have you seen?” This is a powerful rhetorical device. Allah ﷻ isn’t asking for information; rather, He’s drawing attention to something the Prophet ﷺ has witnessed repeatedly. It’s Allah’s way of saying: “You know exactly who I’m talking about. You’ve seen this behavior all too much.”

The use of alladhee (the one who) rather than man (someone who) adds specificity while maintaining strategic ambiguity. It’s like saying “I know exactly who they are” without naming names. This pointed yet unnamed reference makes the criticism both universal and particular - it applies to specific individuals in Makkah while establishing a timeless principle.

Understanding Yukadhdhib

The word (يُكَذِّبُ) comes from the root kadhaba (to lie), but in its intensive form (fa’ala pattern) carries multiple dimensions:

  1. To call something a lie - actively declaring the deen to be false
  2. To consider something a lie - internally believing it’s false
  3. To lie against something - fabricating false claims about the deen

This isn’t passive disbelief. The one who yukadhdhib is a proactive enemy of the faith, actively campaigning against it. In the context of Makkah, such people weren’t just denying Islam’s truth; they were spreading false claims - “He’s a magician,” “He’s possessed,” “He’s a poet gone mad.” This is takdheeb - active propagation of lies against the religion.

Three Meanings of Deen

The word (ٱلدِّينِ) carries three interconnected meanings, all relevant here:

1. The Day of Judgment

Deen can refer to Yawm ad-Deen - the Day of Recompense, the ultimate Day of Justice. Some scholars, including companions, understood this ayah to describe those who deny resurrection and the final accountability. This interpretation makes profound sense when connected to the next ayah about pushing away the orphan.

Think about it: Why would someone have the audacity to mistreat a defenseless orphan? Because they don’t believe anyone will hold them accountable. No human authority will question them - the orphan has no protector, no influential family. The only One who would hold them accountable is Allah ﷻ on the Day of Judgment. If you deny that day, you deny the only real consequence for your cruelty. The two concepts are logically inseparable.

2. The Religion of Islam

Deen also means the religion itself - Islam, the true path that Allah ﷻ revealed. This meaning is particularly cutting when we remember who is being criticized: the custodians of the Kaaba, the people who inherited the legacy of Ibrahim (AS).

The Kaaba is the heart of the global Muslim community. Like blood circulating from the heart throughout the body, the teachings of true deen were meant to spread from the Kaaba to all humanity. The Quraysh were supposed to be the source, the custodians, the ambassadors of this mercy to mankind. Instead, they have become its fiercest enemies. The irony is devastating: those closest to the center of true worship have strayed the furthest from its spirit.

3. Justice Itself

At its most basic level, deen relates to dealings, transactions, fairness - the concept of justice itself. The one who yukadhdhib bid-deen has no sense of justice, no concept of right and wrong in how they treat others. This meaning flows naturally into the subsequent ayahs about mistreatment of the vulnerable.

Historical Context: Abu Lahab

Some companions, when they heard this surah, immediately thought of Abu Lahab. Understanding why illuminates the ayah’s power.

Abu Lahab was in charge of the finances and fundraising around the Kaaba. As a temple in their eyes, the Kaaba had funds for maintenance, charity, and community needs. Abu Lahab controlled this money - and was notorious for embezzling it for personal use. When the poor and orphans came seeking their rightful assistance, he would aggressively push them away. His temper was legendary; people feared approaching him. His name itself - meaning “Father of the Flame” - reflected his explosive anger.

Yet this surah doesn’t start by criticizing his theft or his cruelty. It starts by identifying him as one who denies the deen. This is Allah’s assessment: regardless of his official position as custodian of the sacred house, his behavior reveals his true spiritual state. He is an enemy of true religion.

However, we must be careful. When a companion thought of Abu Lahab hearing these ayahs, that doesn’t mean the ayah is “about” Abu Lahab exclusively. When we hear a khutbah describing a hypocrite or a tyrant, someone might come to our mind - hopefully ourselves first, but perhaps someone we know. That’s a natural human response to hearing descriptions.

For the Sahaba (RA), Abu Lahab or Abu Jahl came to mind when hearing these ayahs. But that’s their insight into how they understood the Quran, not a limitation of the Quran’s meaning. To restrict the infinite wisdom of the Quran to one person’s finite understanding - even a Sahabi’s understanding - would be to cage the Quran’s timeless message. We appreciate their insights while recognizing that the Quran’s guidance extends far beyond any single historical figure.

Ayah 2

فَذَٰلِكَ ٱلَّذِى يَدُعُّ ٱلْيَتِيمَ

“For that is the one who drives away the orphan.”

The Devastating Connection

The particle fa at the beginning establishes this as a consequence or clarification of what came before. It’s as if Allah ﷻ is saying: “You want to know who denies the deen? Let Me tell you exactly who that is.”

Dhalika (that one) points back emphatically. This isn’t a separate category of people. The one who denies resurrection, who rejects true Islam, who has no sense of justice - that very person is identified by one clear sign: how they treat the orphan.

The Meaning of Yadu’

The verb (يَدُعُّ) means to push forcefully, to drive away, to shove aside. It’s not merely neglect; it’s active rejection. But this pushing can take multiple forms:

Physical pushing: The orphan comes seeking help, and they are literally shoved away, turned away at the door.

Structural pushing: Setting up systems, offices, and palaces in ways that make the orphan inaccessible. You push them away without ever having to see them or physically touch them. The barrier is built into the system itself.

Cultural pushing: Creating a society where helping the vulnerable is seen as foolish, where people are discouraged from giving. Remember the people of the garden in Surah Al-Qalam who decided to harvest before dawn specifically to avoid the poor people who would be waiting for their share.

All of these forms of yadu’ are encompassed in this criticism.

Why the Orphan?

The specific mention of (ٱلْيَتِيمَ) is profound. An orphan is a child who has lost their parents - their natural protectors, providers, and advocates in society. They are the most vulnerable members of any community, completely dependent on the mercy and justice of others.

If you believe in the Day of Judgment, you know that Allah ﷻ is the ultimate protector of the orphan. You know you will be held accountable for how you treated them. But if you deny resurrection, if you deny ultimate accountability, then who’s going to question you? The orphan has no powerful family to defend them, no influential tribe to demand justice. You can push them with impunity - unless you believe in Allah’s justice.

This is why the two ayahs are inseparably linked. Denial of resurrection inevitably leads to cruelty toward the vulnerable.

The Prophet’s Personal Connection

This makes even more sense when we remember that the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was himself an orphan. Allah ﷻ reminded him in Surah Ad-Duha: (أَلَمْ يَجِدْكَ يَتِيمًا فَـَٔاوَىٰ) - “Did He not find you an orphan and give you refuge?”

Having experienced that vulnerability himself, the Prophet ﷺ was then commanded in the same surah: (فَأَمَّا ٱلْيَتِيمَ فَلَا تَقْهَرْ) - “As for the orphan, do not oppress them.”

Allah ﷻ found him as an orphan and gave him refuge. Now that he has been given security and honor, he must ensure no other orphan experiences the oppression and rejection he could have faced. This is one of the fundamental teachings (sunan) of our faith: care for these children is a core priority of Islam.

To allow orphans to be sidelined, ignored, or have their needs dismissed is a massive crime in Allah’s sight. The word yateem specifically refers to children who have lost their parents, not adults who were orphaned. This emphasis on children’s welfare is central to the Islamic worldview.

Modern Applications

This teaching demands we examine our conscience today. When we go for Hajj and Umrah, do we find the presence of orphans, of desperately poor children, annoying? Do we walk past them irritably, treating them as a nuisance rather than as human beings deserving of dignity and care?

I witnessed something that broke my heart near the cave of Hira. There was a beautiful masjid where a lengthy khutbah was being delivered about avoiding innovations in the rituals of Hajj - important knowledge, certainly. But when I stepped outside, there were children with visible disabilities - missing limbs, severe injuries - trying to sell calendars to survive.

Inside: a beautifully decorated masjid, pilgrims learning to perfect their rituals. Outside: mutilated children struggling to survive by selling calendars.

How are we performing our Hajj properly when this reality exists outside our door? When we’re about to go enjoy a buffet at our comfortable camps while these children exist in such conditions?

This was the exact criticism Allah ﷻ made of the Quraysh when they were custodians of the House. Are these realities not coming alive in our conscience? They must. And this isn’t just criticism of one government or regime - it’s a question for all of us. We are all going for Hajj; the entire Ummah is responsible.

How do we treat orphans in our own societies? I come from Pakistan - I know how poor children are treated. Children who live in substandard housing, who work odd jobs like shining shoes or running errands, who beg on the streets - how are they treated? That is yadu’ul yateem. If we allow orphans to be pushed around, marginalized, and mistreated, that can only result from the spiritual state described in the first ayah: yukadhdhib bid-deen.

From Allah’s perspective, this behavior is an active campaign against the deen itself. When the Prophet ﷺ was told (وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَٰكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِّلْعَٰلَمِينَ) - “We have not sent you except as a mercy to all the worlds” - part of that mercy was that he himself was an orphan, and he understood their pain.

Care for these children is not optional. It’s not charity. It’s fundamental to having any real deen at all.

Ayah 3

وَلَا يَحُضُّ عَلَىٰ طَعَامِ ٱلْمِسْكِينِ

“And does not encourage the feeding of the poor.”

Understanding Yahuddu

The verb (يَحُضُّ) means to encourage, incite, or urge someone toward action. The root hadda can mean to encourage someone to walk faster, to sell more, to try harder - to push them toward something positive. In this context, it means encouraging others to give, to help, to care.

This person doesn’t just refuse to help themselves - they don’t even encourage others to help. They never say, “We should feed the poor. We should take care of these people. Someone should do something about this.” They remain completely silent about the injustice happening around them.

Active vs. Passive Crimes

Notice the progression in these first three ayahs:

  • Ayah 1: A spiritual crime - denying the deen
  • Ayah 2: An active crime against humanity - pushing away the orphan
  • Ayah 3: A passive crime against humanity - failing to encourage helping the poor

The first ayah seems to be about wronging Allah ﷻ, while the next two are about wronging people. But they’re inseparably connected. How you treat Allah’s creation reveals your true relationship with Allah ﷻ.

The progression also shows that there are two dimensions to injustice: 1. Active oppression: Those who directly harm the vulnerable (yadu’ul yateem) 2. Silent complicity: Those who watch injustice happen and don’t even speak up (la yahuddu)

Both are condemned. You can be guilty not just by what you do, but by what you fail to say.

Who is the Miskeen?

The word (ٱلْمِسْكِينِ) is a four-letter root, often formed by combining two three-letter roots. In this case, it appears to combine concepts from masaka and sakana, both relating to being stuck, held back, or restrained.

A miskeen is someone trapped in their circumstances, unable to help themselves: - Someone who cannot find work and cannot get hired - Someone who is disabled in a way that prevents them from earning - Someone imprisoned or drowning in debt they cannot escape - Someone so sick they cannot provide for themselves

These people are stuck - the very structure of the word suggests their inability to change their situation. They need others to reach out and help them.

The Profound Meaning of Ta’am

This is where the ayah becomes especially powerful. The word used is (طَعَامِ), not (إطعام).

It’am means “feeding” - it’s a verb, an action. Ta’am means “food” - it’s a noun, an object.

When you say “the food of the miskeen” (ta’am il-miskeen), whose food is it? It belongs to the miskeen. When you give food to the poor, you’re not giving them something of yours - you’re giving them what is actually theirs. You’re delivering their right, not doing them a favor.

This is why the Quran says: (ٱلَّذِينَ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَّعْلُومٌ) - “Those in whose wealth there is a recognized right.” If it’s a haqq (right), then it’s not charity or generosity on your part. Just as you have a right to your food, your clothes, your car, they have a right to that provision. You’re not giving them something of yours; you’re giving them something that belongs to them.

Two Dimensions of Injustice

This ayah can be understood on multiple levels:

First: These vulnerable people deserve care and should have their basic needs met, but there is corruption in society. Money owed to them is not delivered. Services promised are not provided. Society systematically deprives them of their rights, and we don’t even talk about this deprivation. We don’t encourage addressing it.

Think of modern parallels: - Veterans who served their country but are denied basic medical care or housing - Elderly people who paid into pension systems their entire working lives, only to have those pensions cut or eliminated - Workers whose retirement accounts are mismanaged or stolen - Any group promised protection by society but then abandoned

That was their ta’am - their rightful provision. It was theirs to begin with. When it’s taken away, they’re in a state of miskana (being stuck), and you’re not even encouraging people to restore what they’re owed.

Second: Beyond systemic injustice, even on a personal level, this ayah challenges us. Do we encourage giving within our own sphere of influence?

Picture this: You’re at a restaurant with your children, enjoying good food. A child walks by who clearly needs food. This is a moment to teach your child something profound: “Here, take your plate. Go give it to him. Give him a hug and this plate.”

“But it’s my ice cream!”

“No, you can have ice cream many times. He’s never had any. Go give him some and show him kindness.”

This is yahuddu - encouraging giving within your own family, teaching the next generation to care.

The Opposite Behavior

What do we find instead? A family member rolls down the car window to give something to a beggar, and someone else quickly intervenes: “Don’t do that! Don’t give to those people. If you give to one, twenty more will come next time. Don’t be stupid.”

La yahuddu ’ala ta’am il-miskeen. Not only do they not give themselves, they actively discourage others from giving. They create a culture where helping the vulnerable is seen as foolish, naive, or enabling bad behavior.

Personal Reflection

We have to examine ourselves: Do we do this daily? Do we encourage the feeding and care of the miskeen? Or do we participate in a culture that dismisses their needs, that trains people to look away, that hardens hearts against those who are suffering?

The Quraysh were supposed to be the world’s greatest example of generosity. Allah ﷻ had blessed them with more wealth than they could ever need for themselves. When Allah ﷻ gives you abundance, what should you do? Share it abundantly. Be more charitable. The more Allah ﷻ gives you, the more giving you should become.

If that principle is true, then the Quraysh - with all their wealth from trade, their privileged position, their economic prosperity - should have been the ultimate example of generosity for all humanity. Instead, they became the ultimate example of greed. Where they should have been the world’s most charitable, they became the world’s most miserly.

The Connection to Faith

Notice again how these ayahs connect spiritual reality with social behavior. You cannot separate the two. If someone claims to believe in judgment, claims to follow true religion, claims to value justice - but doesn’t care for the vulnerable and doesn’t even encourage others to care - their claim is hollow.

Care for humanity is at the very heart of iman. If that care isn’t there, nothing else is there. This is what should break our hearts when we see Muslims who are deeply devoted to ritual but completely indifferent to human suffering.

True worship of the Lord of the House cannot exist alongside indifference to the orphan and the miskeen. These ayahs make that impossible to ignore.

Ayah 4

فَوَيْلٌ لِّلْمُصَلِّينَ

“So woe to those who pray.”

The Shocking Transition

This ayah marks the structural shift in the surah. We move from singular to plural, from one type of criticism to another. But the transition is shocking: waylun lil-musalleen - destruction to those who pray.

Wait. Woe to people who pray? This seems contradictory. Isn’t prayer the very foundation of our faith? How can Allah ﷻ condemn people for praying?

Understanding Wayl

The word (وَيْلٌ) is the same word used in Surah Al-Humazah: (وَيْلٌ لِّكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍۢ لُّمَزَةٍ) - “Woe to every slanderer and backbiter.”

Wayl indicates the most terrible form of destruction, violent death, complete devastation. It’s a deliberate echo of that earlier surah, but notice the striking difference:

  • In Surah Al-Humazah: Wayl for those who hurt other people (through slander and backbiting)
  • In Surah Al-Ma’un: Wayl for those who wrong Allah ﷻ (through corrupted prayer)

The first three ayahs of Al-Ma’un dealt with crimes against humanity. Now we shift to crimes against Allah ﷻ - yet the punishment announced is just as severe. This should give us pause.

The Historical Context

Imagine the scene: The Prophet ﷺ is standing by the Kaaba, surrounded by idols, with the Quraysh performing their own version of prayer. And he recites: Fa waylun lil-musalleen - “Woe to those who pray.”

“We’re all praying! You’re just criticizing us like that?”

The Quran itself describes what their “prayer” had become: (وَمَا كَانَ صَلَاتُهُمْ عِندَ ٱلْبَيْتِ إِلَّا مُكَآءً وَتَصْدِيَةً) - “Their prayer at the House was nothing but whistling and clapping.” They had turned worship into empty ritual and performance.

But this ayah goes deeper. It’s not just about their corrupted rituals. It’s about something that applies to any person in any time who prays: What is the state of your heart regarding prayer itself?

The Reversal of Proportion

Notice something crucial about the structure. In the first half of the surah: - One crime against Allah ﷻ (yukadhdhib bid-deen) - Two crimes against humanity (yadu’ul yateem, la yahuddu)

In the second half: - Two crimes against Allah ﷻ (the next two ayahs about prayer) - One crime against humanity (the final ayah about preventing ma’un)

The proportions are flipped. This creates a beautiful symmetry while emphasizing that both dimensions - our relationship with Allah ﷻ and our treatment of people - are inseparably linked and equally important.

Why This Applies to the Quraysh

Someone might ask: “This criticism about prayer and showing off seems to describe hypocrites, and hypocrisy is a phenomenon of Madinah, not Makkah. Doesn’t this prove the surah is Madani?”

Not necessarily. The Quraysh were custodians of the House of Allah ﷻ. They had their own version of prayer, their own rituals. Even those rituals - corrupted as they were - weren’t done with sincerity. Even their modified, invented forms of worship were performed superficially, without genuine devotion.

The criticism lands even more powerfully when we realize: These are the people society views as the most religious. They’re the custodians of the Kaaba. Everyone assumes their prayer must be exemplary. They make great public displays of their devotion during festivals and ceremonies. But Allah ﷻ exposes the reality: Even that is hollow.

A Warning About All Worship

This ayah establishes a principle that extends far beyond prayer: Any act you do that’s supposed to be for Allah ﷻ - if you do it without sincerity, without care, primarily for show - then wayl for you. The destruction promised here isn’t for those who don’t pray. It’s for those who pray incorrectly, with the wrong heart, for the wrong reasons.

That’s more dangerous than not praying at all.

Ayah 5

ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَن صَلَاتِهِمْ سَاهُونَ

“Those who are heedless of their prayer.”

A Crucial Clarification

This ayah clarifies the previous one. Not all who pray are condemned - only those with a specific characteristic: sahoon regarding their prayer.

Understanding Sahoon

The word (سَاهُونَ) comes from (سهو), which belongs to the category of words meaning to forget, to be unmindful, to be heedless. Your heart and mind are not engaged. You’re not really paying attention. It’s not important to you.

But notice the preposition: ’an salatihim - not fee salatihim.

This distinction is critical. If the ayah said fee salatihim sahoon, it would mean: “Those who, while inside their prayer, experience moments of forgetfulness or distraction.” That would be a huge problem for all of us, because who hasn’t had their mind wander during prayer? Who hasn’t thought about dinner or tomorrow’s meeting during salah? If that was the meaning, we’d all be condemned.

But Allah ﷻ said ’an salatihim - “about their prayer” or “regarding their prayer.” This refers to their overall attitude toward prayer itself, not what happens inside the prayer. They’re heedless about:

  • Whether they pray or not
  • When they pray
  • The time of prayer
  • The importance of prayer
  • The obligation of prayer

Prayer just isn’t a big deal to them.

The Meaning of Salatihim

Another crucial detail: (صَلَاتِهِمْ) - “their prayer.”

Not as-salah (the prayer), but “their prayer.” This possessive form acknowledges that the Quraysh had their own version of prayer. They had modified it, corrupted it, turned it into their own ritual. But even that modified version - whatever prayer they did perform - even that wasn’t important to them.

If you’re serious about your religion, whatever your concept of worship is, that worship should matter to you. Missing it should be a big deal. Being late should concern you. The Quraysh weren’t even serious about their own corrupted version.

Broader Application

While this was revealed about the Quraysh, the principle applies universally. What is our attitude toward prayer?

When we miss a prayer, is it a big deal? Or do we think, “Well, there’s always another one”? When the time for prayer is running out, do we rush to pray, or do we think, “Eh, I’ll catch it later”? When we’re about to pray, do we prepare our hearts, or do we just go through the motions?

Sahoon means prayer isn’t really serious to us. We’re loose about it. We’re casual. It’s not actually important in our hearts, even if we technically still do it sometimes.

Why Prayer Matters

Think back to the beginning of this discussion. The entire premise of deen Ibrahim - the religion of Ibrahim (AS) - is having a sound, sincere heart: (قَلْبٌ سَلِيمٌ). The whole point of the Kaaba, the whole point of the rituals around it, was to create that sincerity.

Allah ﷻ chose the Kaaba’s location specifically: barren, isolated, no ocean view, no pleasant weather, no palm trees, no worldly attractions. When you go there, you can’t be distracted by the beauty of the world. You’re there solely for Allah ﷻ. The location itself is meant to strip away everything else and focus the heart purely on Him.

The Quraysh lived around the Kaaba. They were blessed to be near this place constantly. If going to the Kaaba increases our iman when we visit, imagine the level of iman they should have had, living there permanently. Their spiritual temperature should have been blazing hot with devotion.

Instead, Allah ﷻ reveals: alladheen hum ’an salatihim sahoon. What a tragedy. When you pray, you don’t really care about that prayer. Even if you do it, you’re not genuinely there.

A Personal Reality Check

This ayah forces us to examine ourselves honestly:

  • When the adhan is called, is our first thought about prayer, or about finishing what we’re doing first?
  • When we pray, are we genuinely present with Allah ﷻ, or just physically going through motions while mentally elsewhere?
  • When we miss a prayer, does it bother us deeply, or do we easily shrug it off?

These questions reveal whether we have sahw regarding our salah.

The ayah isn’t primarily about what happens inside the prayer (though that matters too). It’s about our fundamental attitude: Is prayer actually important to us? Is it a priority? Does it mean something?

If we’re heedless about it, if we’re casual about it, if we treat it like an optional formality - then we’re manifesting the very quality this ayah condemns.

Ayah 6

ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ يُرَآءُونَ

“Those who make show [of their deeds].”

The Second Crime Against Allah ﷻ

After describing their heedlessness about prayer, Allah ﷻ now describes what happens when they do pray: yura’oon - they do it for show.

Understanding Yura’oon

The word (يُرَآءُونَ) comes from the root (رأى), meaning “to see.” The word (رِيَاء) means showing off, doing something to be seen. But this particular form is profound.

Yura’oon is a mufa’ala form, which implies reciprocal action. It’s not just that they’re showing something - they’re showing and expecting something to be shown back to them. Let me explain:

When you do an act of worship for show, you’re doing it so others will see. But that’s only half of riya’. The other half is: you desperately want to see their reaction. You want to gauge their response. Did they notice? Were they impressed? Did they admire me? Did they praise me? Did they acknowledge what I did?

Yura’oon means: “I’m showing you something, and I’m constantly checking - what are you showing me back? What’s your facial expression? What did you say? What’s the reaction on social media? How many people saw it? How many people liked it?”

It’s a cycle: showing and checking the response, constantly.

Beyond Prayer

The ayah says alladheen hum yura’oon - not specifically “they show off in their prayer,” just “they show off.” This broadens the scope beyond salah to any act supposedly done for Allah ﷻ.

Think of it comprehensively: - Any ritual you perform - Any good deed you do
- Any act of worship - Any display of piety

When you do these things in ways calculated to maximize visibility, and then you keep checking for reactions and validation - that’s riya’.

The person consumed by riya’ is always strategizing: How can I do this so more people see? What will get the most attention? How can I make sure people know what I did for the sake of Allah ﷻ?

And after doing it, they’re consumed with checking responses: What did people say? How many people noticed? What was the feedback? Did they give me credit? Did they appreciate me?

The Quraysh Specifically

In the context of the Quraysh, this criticism is devastating. They were the custodians of the Kaaba. Everyone viewed them as the most religious people in society. They had to maintain that image, so they made grand public displays of their devotion.

But Allah ﷻ exposes the reality: alladheen hum ’an salatihim sahoon - They don’t actually care about prayer. Alladheen hum yura’oon - They only do it when people are watching, for appearances, to maintain their reputation.

This is eerily similar to modern politicians who visit houses of worship during election season with camera crews in tow. Are they interested in worship? In connecting with God? In genuine spirituality? No. They need the religious vote, so they need to appear pious. They need to look like “people of God.”

Yura’oon perfectly describes that behavior: religious performance for political purposes.

The Modern Mirror

This ayah should make us deeply uncomfortable because riya’ is so easy in the social media age.

We can post our good deeds, our prayers, our charity, our Islamic activities - and then refresh obsessively to see the response. We can carefully craft our image of piety for public consumption while our private reality is entirely different.

The question isn’t whether we do acts of worship. The question is: Why? For Whom? And what do we do afterward?

If your heart is consumed with “Did people see what I did? Did they appreciate it? What was the reaction?” - that’s riya’. You’re showing, and you’re desperately seeking validation in return.

The Opposite of Sincerity

Remember: The whole point of true deen, the religion of Ibrahim (AS), is qalbun saleem - a sound, sincere heart. The Kaaba was built to facilitate that sincerity.

Riya’ is the antithesis of sincerity. You’re not doing it for Allah ﷻ. You’re doing it for people, for status, for validation, for reputation. And Allah ﷻ is exposing that hollow performance.

Two Crimes Against Allah ﷻ Connected

Notice the progression:

First: ’an salatihim sahoon - They don’t really care about prayer Second: yura’oon - When they do pray, it’s only for show

These two characteristics go together. If prayer isn’t important to you internally, the only time you’ll do it is when it’s socially beneficial - when others are watching, when it improves your image, when it serves your worldly interests.

People whose hearts are genuinely devoted to Allah ﷻ pray whether anyone sees them or not. They pray in private as fervently as in public. They’re not performing; they’re worshipping.

But people whose hearts are not actually connected to Allah ﷻ only perform the rituals when there’s a worldly benefit to doing so. That’s the state of these people.

Ayah 7

وَيَمْنَعُونَ ٱلْمَاعُونَ

“And withhold [simple] assistance.”

The Final Crime

After two ayahs about crimes against Allah ﷻ (heedlessness about prayer and showing off), the surah concludes with a crime against humanity: withholding ma’un.

Understanding Al-Ma’un

(ٱلْمَاعُونَ) is one of the most beautiful and comprehensive words in this surah. Classical scholars defined it as: kullu shay’in ma’ruf - “every small, common thing.” Some said: al-qidr was-sikanah wal-fa’s - “a pot, a knife, a small tool.”

Ma’un refers to simple items, small favors, minor assistance - the kind of help you wouldn’t normally think twice about giving:

  • Can I borrow a pot?
  • Can I use your phone?
  • Can you give me a ride?
  • Can you spare $5?
  • Can you help me carry this?
  • Can I get some gas money?

These are small things, trivial to the one who has them, but potentially crucial to the one who needs them. Ma’un is precisely that: minor items and small assistance that should flow naturally between people.

Yamna’oon - They Prevent

The verb (يَمْنَعُونَ) means to prevent, to withhold, to create barriers. But notice it’s not just that they personally don’t give ma’un. They prevent it - meaning they create systems, cultures, and mindsets where nobody gives ma’un to anybody.

They want to establish a society where everyone is radically individualistic, where everyone looks out only for themselves, where helping others is seen as weakness or foolishness.

Why Create This Culture?

Why would they want this? Because when everyone is isolated and focused only on their own survival, people don’t unite. When hearts are hard toward one another, when people don’t help each other even with small things, they certainly won’t come together to demand justice or challenge those in power.

The powerful benefit from a fragmented, individualistic society where people are too busy or too selfish to organize for the common good. So they don’t just refuse to help - they actively prevent a culture of helping from emerging.

Think about it: In an apartment building in a major city, people can live next door to each other for years and never exchange a word, never help each other, never even know each other’s names. Everyone minds their own business. Nobody helps anybody.

When that mindset dominates, when that culture is established, then you can have devastating poverty down the street and nobody cares. You can have orphans (yateem) suffering, you can have people who desperately need help (la yahuddu ’ala ta’amil miskeen) - and hearts remain completely unmoved.

Why? Because the culture of yamna’oon al-ma’un has been successfully established. Hearts have been hardened. Individualism has triumphed over community care.

The Heart Won’t Beat

When this culture exists, the heart of society stops beating. Compassion dies. Even if someone is homeless down the street, even if children are suffering visible poverty, nobody sheds a tear. Why? Because the culture of ma’un has been systematically prevented.

The Connection to Everything Before

Look at how this final ayah connects to everything:

From the first half: - They pushed away the orphan (yadu’ul yateem) - They didn’t encourage feeding the poor (la yahuddu ’ala ta’amil miskeen)
- Now: They prevent even small assistance (yamna’oon al-ma’un)

From the second half: - They’re heedless about prayer (’an salatihim sahoon) - They show off in worship (yura’oon) - Now: They prevent ma’un

How Are These Connected?

The genius of this surah is how Allah ﷻ connects things we don’t normally connect.

Question: How do you know if someone’s prayer is just for show? How can you possibly tell? You can’t see inside their heart.

Answer: Look at how they treat people in small, everyday interactions. Look at whether they withhold ma’un.

Someone truly close to Allah ﷻ, someone whose heart is genuinely soft toward their Creator - that person will have the softest heart toward creation. They can’t help it. When your heart is connected to Allah ﷻ, your heart naturally extends mercy and kindness to His creation.

Conversely, when you find someone who is miserly about ma’un, who withholds even small helps, who creates a culture of selfishness - that’s a pretty reliable indicator that their religiosity is artificial. Their heart isn’t really with Allah ﷻ, regardless of how pious they appear.

We see this principle in other places in the Quran. Consider Surah Al-Kawthar (which we’ll study next): (فَصَلِّ لِرَبِّكَ وَٱنْحَرْ) - “So pray to your Lord and sacrifice.”

One act is for Allah ﷻ (prayer). One act is for people (the meat from sacrifice goes to people). They’re mentioned together because they go together. That’s how you show gratitude to Allah ﷻ - by worshipping Him and by caring for His creation.

The Comprehensive Criticism

Surah Al-Ma’un comprehensively criticizes the Quraysh as being neither good to Allah ﷻ nor good to people:

Crimes against Allah ﷻ: - They deny the deen (yukadhdhib bid-deen) - They’re heedless about prayer (’an salatihim sahoon) - They show off in worship (yura’oon)

Crimes against humanity: - They push away orphans (yadu’ul yateem) - They don’t encourage feeding the poor (la yahuddu ’ala ta’amil miskeen) - They prevent even small assistance (yamna’oon al-ma’un)

They’re no good to either one. This is the opposite of true ibadah.

Conclusion: The Disqualification of Quraysh

The Broken Covenant

In Surah Quraysh, Allah ﷻ blessed the Quraysh with enormous favors: - Ilaf - habitual safety and security in their travels
- Freedom from hunger - Freedom from fear

He gave them the Kaaba. He gave them economic prosperity. He gave them the most honored position in Arabian society.

Why? Falya’budu Rabba hadhal bayt - “So that they should worship the Lord of this House.”

But this surah - Surah Al-Ma’un - proves beyond any doubt that they have utterly failed. They haven’t held up their end of the covenant. The favors Allah ﷻ gave them were meant to make them the greatest example of: - Faith in judgment (yukadhdhib bid-deen - they deny it instead) - Mercy to orphans (yadu’ul yateem - they push them away instead)
- Generosity (la yahuddu - they don’t even encourage it instead) - Sincere worship (sahoon, yura’oon - they’re heedless and showing off instead) - Community care (yamna’oon al-ma’un - they prevent it instead)

They were supposed to inherit the legacy of Ibrahim (AS), whose heart was so soft that when angels came to destroy the nation of Lut (AS), he argued with them: (يُجَٰدِلُنَا فِى قَوْمِ لُوطٍۢ) - “He argued with Us about the people of Lut.”

That same Ibrahim (AS) built the Kaaba. His concern was always for humanity’s wellbeing. If the Quraysh were truly followers of his religion, how could they be so cruel to the most deserving of mercy - the orphans, the poor, the vulnerable?

The Question That Follows

If the Quraysh are disqualified, if they’ve proven themselves unworthy custodians of the Kaaba, then a question arises:

Who should be the custodian of the Kaaba?

That question is answered in the next surah we’ll study: (إِنَّآ أَعْطَيْنَٰكَ ٱلْكَوْثَرَ) - “Indeed, We have granted you abundance.”

Allah ﷻ switches the authority and honor to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. The responsibility passes to him. The Quraysh had their chance and squandered it. Now true worship of the Lord of the House will be established through a new custodian.

For Us Today

But before we move forward, we must sit with this surah’s mirror turned toward ourselves.

These criticisms weren’t just for the ancient Quraysh. The Kaaba was the center, and everything else - every masjid, every Muslim home, every Muslim community - is an extension of that center. Barakna hawlaha - “We blessed around it.” We’re in those blessed surroundings.

That means we carry the same responsibilities. In our communities, in our societies, wherever Muslims live as minorities, we have the role the Quraysh were supposed to fulfill. We’re meant to be ambassadors of this mercy, custodians of this guidance.

The spirit of the Kaaba should be alive in every masjid, in every Muslim home. So we must ask ourselves:

About faith in accountability: - Do we really believe in the Day of Judgment? - Does that belief shape how we treat the vulnerable?

About orphans and the vulnerable: - How do we treat children who have no parents? - Do we push them away literally or systemically? - Are we creating systems that care for the most vulnerable, or systems that marginalize them?

About encouraging good: - When we see injustice, do we speak up? - Do we encourage giving and helping in our families and communities? - Or do we teach our children to “look out for number one”?

About prayer: - Is prayer really important to us, or just a cultural identity marker? - When we miss salah, does it bother us deeply? - Do we pray the same way alone as we do in public?

About sincerity: - Are we performing religiosity for social validation? - Do we constantly check for reactions to our “good deeds”? - Are we building a careful image while our private reality is different?

About small kindnesses: - Do we give ma’un freely - small helps, simple assistance? - Or do we withhold even minor things out of selfishness? - Are we creating a culture of care or a culture of individualism?

The Heart of Worship

What this surah ultimately teaches is that true ibadah - true servitude to the Lord of the House - cannot be separated from care for humanity.

You cannot wrong people and claim to be right with Allah ﷻ. You cannot be cruel to creation and claim closeness to the Creator. You cannot push away the orphan and say you believe in Judgment. You cannot withhold ma’un and claim your prayer is sincere.

The two are inseparable. This is the heart of the deen of Ibrahim (AS). This is what it means to be a true servant of Allah ﷻ.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from being among those described in this surah. May He make our hearts soft toward Him and toward His creation. May He make us people who truly care for the orphan, encourage helping the needy, maintain sincere prayer, and freely give even small assistance to others.

And may He make us worthy custodians of His message, worthy inheritors of the legacy of His prophets (AS), and worthy servants of the Lord of the House.

Ameen.
